result:
The first thing that comes is the hum, the low, almost-sub-audible thrum that lives behind the sternum when the house is quiet and the city beyond the window has surrendered its neon sigh. I used to think the hum was loneliness, but loneliness has a sharper edge; this is more like a companion fog, a second bloodstream. It carries every word I never said in the right key, every glance I returned too late, every letter I started in my head at 3:07 a.m. and exhaled into the dark like cigarette smoke I never inhaled.  

Tonight the fog tastes of cedar and static electricity, the way December always does when the cold has forgotten how to snow. I keep remembering the Christmas my mother wrapped the cat—alive, indignant—in blinking lights because she’d run out of tinsel. We laughed until we sounded like cracked bells, and somewhere in that laughter was the first hint that joy could be weaponized, could leave bruises shaped like tinsel-cuts on soft orange fur. The cat lived seventeen more years; the laughter only eight months. After that it turned into something we quoted rather than felt, like a foreign proverb no one could translate back into the original tongue.

I have been trying to reconstruct the original tongue.

Yesterday I found a VHS tape at the thrift store labeled only “Sarah – 1994.” I don’t know any Sarah; I bought it anyway, because I have a theory that every orphaned image wants to be watched the way every ghost wants to be named. The tape was brittle, the plastic case cracked like glacier lines. The kid at the counter shrugged when I asked if they had a player—“Try the attic of any house built before Y2K,” he said, already scrolling his phone, already somewhere else. So I carried Sarah home like contraband memory, cradled her against the faint diesel smell of the bus. Maybe she contains a tenth-birthday party where the magician’s dove refused to reappear. Maybe she’s a prom queen blinking under crepe paper constellations, or a grandmother proofreading recipes on the kitchen counter, voice thick with Pall Mall and premonition. Whatever she is, she once mattered enough to press Record, and that alone makes her sacred.

We forget, constantly, that recording is an act of prayer: Please let this moment be bigger than my mouth.  

I keep thinking about mouths. My partner’s mouth when it shapes the sound oh in surprise, the O becoming a planet I want to colonize. The mouth of the homeless man on Lexington who once told me, “The universe is just a rumor atoms spread about god,” then laughed so hard he coughed up a petal of blood. The mouth of the child I wasn’t—still isn’t—ready to raise, asking what happens when we die, asking it the way you ask where clouds go at night, certain there’s a cupboard, a hook, a lost-and-found bin. I tell the truth in the shape of a story: we become the quiet parts of songs, the hush between cymbal and snare. The child considers this, then requests a peanut-butter sandwich without crusts, which feels like its own kind of theology.

There are, by actual count, 1,347 books in this apartment. They lean like drunk parishioners against the walls, spines cracked, pages foxed, smelling of basement and pencil shavings. Some nights I pull one down at random and read a single paragraph as if it were horoscope, oracle, prescription. Tonight it was Calvino: “You walk for days among trees and among stones. Rarely does the eye light on anything, but when it does it is a revelation.” I underlined it years ago, but tonight the ink has bled like tired veins and the revelation feels less like lightning than like someone gently closing my hand around a warm mug. I keep forgetting that revelation can be quiet, can be chamomile, can be the way the radiator exhales at 4:17 a.m. exactly when I need to know I am not alone in being awake.

I want to invent a new unit of measurement: the widow-second, defined as the length of time between when a text message says Read and when the three dancing dots reappear. Longer than a second, shorter than a grave. I have wasted entire lunar cycles inside widow-seconds, have grown moss on my north side, have forgotten the difference between patience and surrender.  

But then the dots revive, and the sentence that arrives is ordinary—Running late, save me some soup—and suddenly the cosmos folds into a paper crane and perches on the curtain rod, pleased with its own small weight. Love, it turns out, is mostly logistics: who will buy toilet paper, who will remember the can opener when the power fails, who will kiss the eyelids of the other before the anesthesia takes. I used to think love was the crescendo; now I know it is the pedal sustaining the note while crescendo forgets itself and returns to piano.

Outside, a siren Dopplers past, painting the room red, then not-red. The cat—different cat, same stubborn genes—lifts its head, eyes wide as cancelled coins. Everything in the city is always leaving and returning, a tide of sirens, deliveries, drunk couples harmonizing with the streetlights. I used to want to escape it; now I think escape is just another room inside the same house, a door that opens onto the same hallway wearing a moustache to fool you. The real trick is to stand still long enough to feel the floor remember you, to let the apartment settle on your shoulders like a well-worn coat weighted with keys to doors that no longer exist.

I worry sometimes that I am hoarding nostalgia the way dragon-boarding houses hoard coins, that my cells are filling with Polaroid chemicals that will never again receive light. But then I remember how even film needs the dark to bloom its ghosts, that nostalgia is only toxic when mistaken for map; when used as atmosphere it can be nutrient. So I breathe it diluted: one part 1987, two parts tonight, shake over ice made from yesterday’s tap water, garnish with a sprig of maybe tomorrow.

Tomorrow: a word shaped like a drawer that refuses to close because something soft is caught inside. Tomorrow I will return the library books, call the dentist, find out if Sarah still exists anywhere but magnetic ribbon. Tomorrow I will learn the difference between forgiveness and amnesia. Tomorrow I will drink less coffee, or more; will measure the exact half-life of attention; will apologize to the version of me who believed 1,000 words could fence the wilderness of thought.  

But tonight the wilderness is gentle, prowling the edges of the rug like a moth that thinks the lamp is the moon. Tonight I have 1,000 steps to the kitchen and 1,000 back, enough to memorize the creak in the floorboard that sounds like my father clearing his throat. Tonight the hum in my chest harmonizes with the radiator and the cat’s purr and the faint heartbeat of the neighbors’ stereo through plaster older than my mother.  

I write because I do not know how to end things, only how to pause them. So: pause.  

Take this moment—cedar, static, half-eaten moon, sleeping city, unread VHS, unread heart—and fold it into the smallest possible square. Tuck it under the tongue. Let it dissolve like the Host they promised would turn into galaxies inside us. Walk around with it unswallowed, tasting iron, tasting cedar, tasting 1994 and tomorrow and every widow-second you ever survived. When the next moment comes, open your mouth: the word that escapes is the first line of the next 1,000, the next hum, the next miracle ordinary as soup.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
